content:
Not long ago, when I was working on an Android project, I’ve noticed a problem with a Kotlin function that was using an enum. I needed to get a value from an enum class but because the data was received from an external API I needed to map it based on a value. So the enum class looked something like this:
enum class WeirdEnum(val value: Int) { Zero, One, Two, Seven, Eight, Nine, Ten, Eleven, companion object { private val values = WeirdEnum.values() fun getByValue(value: Int) = values.first {it.ordinal == value} } } But then when I called WeirdEnum.getByValue(7) I actually got a value of WeirdEnum.Eleven instead of WeirdEnum.Seven as was expected. Take a look:
fun main() { val numberOne = WeirdEnum.One val numberSevenByValue = WeirdEnum.getByValue(7) println(numberOne)	// One println(numberSevenByValue) // Eleven } Do you see the it.ordinal call inside the companion object of the WeirdEnum? That was the source of the problem. You see, everything is fine when all the values in the enum used numbers as values with no breaks between them. But here, as you can see, values between 3 and 6 are missing. How can you proceed then?
It turns out it’s an easy fix once you know where to look. All it takes is to update the companion object. I changed the it.ordinal call to a it.value but for the purposes of learning I will leave both versions just to see the differences.
The final code for this enum before and after the change is available as a gist here:
summary: Not long ago, when I was working…
title: Retrieving values from Kotlin enum classes

content:
Hi My name is rogowskibart and I love making mobile and web apps.
For the past 4 years I’ve been learning Java during my studies. I’ve worked as a Java developer for about 2 years. This year, however, I fell in love with Kotlin and I’m thrilled to learn as much about it as I can.
I’ve always dreamed of teaching other people to code and make their own apps. I want to get better at teaching and sharing my knowledge. I’m even thinking about creating my own video courses. However, I need to start somewhere and this blog should be a great outlet. After all, there is no better way of really processing the knowledge than by teaching it to someone.
Please, feel free to ask me any questions via Twitter or via Instagram
